topic: Two coworkers are stuck in an elevator for three hours
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stale air in the elevator was thick enough to chew. Sarah pressed her back against the cold metal wall, her knuckles white where she gripped her briefcase. Every minute dragged like a physical weight, pressing down on her chest until breathing felt shallow and ragged. Across from her, Mark was humming tunelessly, a sound that grated on her nerves like sandpaper. She stared at the illuminated floor numbers, counting the seconds with a meticulous, angry focus, cataloging every tiny imperfection in the brushed steel. When the silence stretched too long, she let out a sharp, involuntary exhale, the sound barely audible over the low hum of the machinery. "This is ridiculous," she muttered, the words tight and laced with undisguised annoyance. She shifted her weight, trying to find a position that didn't feel so trapped, but the cramped space offered no escape.

[story 2]

I kept my gaze fixed on the reflection in the polished doors, watching my own strained expression contort. The fluorescent lights flickered with a maddening rhythm, each stutter a tiny irritant. Mark kept fiddling with his phone, the soft tap-tap-tap of the screen sounding deafening in the confined space. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the ache radiating up into my temples. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation for this pointless delay, but the words caught in my throat, choked by a simmering resentment. I began to pace the tiny distance available, a tight, jerky circuit of frustration. Each step was a testament to my mounting displeasure, my shoulders hiked up near my ears, broadcasting a clear message of profound dissatisfaction with the entire situation.

[story 3]

Three hours. Three hours suspended in this metal box with this person. Liam meticulously checked his watch, the movement sharp and impatient. He didn't look at me, focusing instead on the ceiling panel, tracing the seams with his eyes as if seeking some hidden flaw. I kept my arms crossed over my chest, a barrier erected between myself and the suffocating proximity of him. When he finally sighed, a long, drawn-out sound of exasperation, I flinched almost imperceptibly. "Can we just... acknowledge how absurd this is?" I asked, my voice pitched low, deliberately devoid of warmth. I watched his reaction—the slight tightening around his eyes—and felt a surge of pure irritation. It was a simmering heat behind my eyes, a refusal to simply accept this enforced idleness.
